sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Compare before and after the RIGHT action
before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
after = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation

# Find the player object (black with white dots - id=4)
before_player = [n for n in before['nodes'] if n['id'] == 4][0]
after_player = [n for n in after['nodes'] if n['id'] == 4][0]

print("Before player boundary:", before_player['boundary'])
print("After player boundary:", after_player['boundary'])

# Check all node boundaries for changes
print("\n--- All node changes ---")
for n in after['nodes']:
    bn = [x for x in before['nodes'] if x['id'] == n['id']][0]
    if bn['boundary'] != n['boundary']:
        print(f"  Node {n['id']} ({n['color']}): {bn['boundary']} -> {n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I can see what happened. The player character (black shape with white dots) moved from the left panel to the right panel. Originally it was at cols 18-26, now it's at cols 21-29 (shifted right by 3 columns). The dark gray T-shape that was at cols 36-44 is now at cols 33-41 (shifted left by 3 columns).
So it seems like the two objects swapped positions! The player moved right and the T-shape moved left. This is interesting - maybe the game mechanic is about swapping or moving objects between panels.
Wait, let me reconsider. The player character boundary changed from `[[15, 18], [15, 26], ...]` to `[[15, 21], [15, 29], ...]`. That's a shift of +3 columns. And the T-shape (dark gray) went from cols 36-44 to cols 33-41, a shift of -3 columns.
So pressing RIGHT moved the player character 3 columns right and the T-shape 3 columns left. They seem to have swapped positions across the divider.
Let me try pressing RIGHT again to see if this pattern continues.

# They seem to have swapped positions!
# Let's try RIGHT again to see the pattern

action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
